posts.name: craig v
posts.cooked:
• 1982 Château Léoville Barton - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Julien (12/23/2015)
    My almost Co WOTN. Edged out by a small margin and palate preference for the 2009 Rousseau Charmes Chambertin. That said, this is about everything I wish every bottle of Bordeaux in my cellar would offer. I have about 70 btls of Bordeaux to 300 btls of red Burgundy in my cellar. I enjoy Bordeaux but rarely do I taste one this exceptional. For me, this was “burgundian” with and etherealness I attach to great Burgundy. Classic Cigar box and lead pencil on the nose and palate. Some forest floor too. Really, so placid and resolved. If only all old btls could maintain this level of freshness and yet show these endless layers. Charmed the pants off of me. Great wine in a great place. Ok, I’ll promote it to my CO WOTH along with the stellar Rousseau Thanks Kevin! A recent acquisition to boot. Really appreciate that my buddy Kevin makes sure the wine has the right amount of air, temp and sediment handling of all his wines. (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.cooked: Yes, breathing time and serving temperature can make a huge difference with a (potentially) very fine older wine. '82 was one of those “perfect” Bordeaux vintages it seems. Hard to think of any other vintage (in the Left Bank) that delivers so much at present. I have some '70s that are still on the young side (!), likewise the '78s, and '86s. The '85s should be about ready but I haven’t opened any recently.

posts.name: Dale Bowers
posts.cooked: 1982 certainly has been the Bordeaux vintage of a lifetime for me.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
A few years ago, I was able to drink this quite frequently, as it was well represented in the cellar of my college. It represents the end of an era, as it was one of the last, I think the last, vintage at Léoville Barton which saw no new oak. The 1985 is cut from a rather different cloth (though I actually rather prefer it).

Since you enjoyed this, you might look to the Langoa 1982. I recollect Anthony Barton remarking that he was inclined to prefer it to its grander sibling in that vintage.

posts.name: Glenn Bienstock
posts.cooked: I love 82 Bordeaux but my lone 82 Leoville Barton about 10 years ago was a case of an old school rough edged bottle that never fully blossomed.

posts.name: craig v
posts.cooked:
LBs can be that way. I have one '96 I’ve been sitting on forever, trying to be patient but I’ll likely roll the dice and drink in the next 1-5yrs.

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked:
I have always found the 1982 Leoville Barton to be a bit on the rustic side (3 bottles I think). Not to say I would not drink it every other night of the year, but not as refined or elegant as their later wines (the first I recollect being really elegant was the 1985).

Otherwise, we had an extensive vertical tasting of Leoville Barton a while back and we found that 20 years was the time to drink (or start drinking), although the slightly younger wines were good. But to venture too much into the younger wines was a problem (e.g., the 2000 LB at the time was pretty much a wasted bottle).

posts.name: craig v
posts.cooked:
Nothing even remotely rustic about this particular '82 and at this age there really shouldn’t be.

posts.cooked:
craig v:

Carl Steefel:

I have always found the 1982 Leoville Barton to be a bit on the rustic side (3 bottles I think). Not to say I would not drink it every other night of the year, but not as refined or elegant as their later wines (the first I recollect being really elegant was the 1985).

Otherwise, we had an extensive vertical tasting of Leoville Barton a while back and we found that 20 years was the time to drink (or start drinking), although the slightly younger wines were good. But to venture too much into the younger wines was a problem (e.g., the 2000 LB at the time was pretty much a wasted bottle).

“Rustic” would refer to the wine making style, not the maturity of the wine. Although I expect that with more age, some of the hall marks (e.g., rough tannins) might continue to fade…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think “rustic” is a pretty fair characterization of this wine—and indeed, of the 1982 Bordeaux vintage in general, outside the confines of the handful of top wines. The 1982 Leoville Barton’s tannins are certainly melting, but it remains quite a broad-shouldered, blocky, chunky and, well, rustic wine. While some of this is a consequence of wine-making at that period (no new oak, less zealous selection, etc), I think some of that character comes from the vintage (cf. the very elegant and refined 1953, cut from a very different cloth, but made in much the same way).

The evolution of more recent vintages of Léoville Barton is an interesting question. I’d suggest that the 1985 and 1988 (well worth looking for) are the best of the decade, followed by the 1989 and 1990 on pretty level pegging. The 1985 is the most attractive recent vintage that is drinking well.

Looking into the 1990s, I had always taken it as given that this estate continued to be one of the arch-traditionalists in the Médoc, but opening a few bottles this summer gave me pause. Up until and including the (very strong) 1998, the wines seem to be very much in the school of the 1985. But from 1999 onwards the wines’ new oak component seemed much more pronounced to me, both in terms of aromatics and tannins (though that can be a hard call to make). That made me wonder if the barrel programme had changed and is marking the wines more than it used to. I’d be curious to hear if anyone has anything to contribute on this subject, admittedly tangential to the thread.

posts.name: craig v
posts.cooked:
Not saying someone wouldn’t hit rusticity in their btl but we experienced a wine with fully resolved and silky tannins. And, frankly, at this btl age I’d consider ANY trace of rusticity a problem and score the wine much lower.

William Kelley:

While some of this is a consequence of wine-making at that period (no new oak, less zealous selection, etc), I think some of that character comes from the vintage (cf. the very elegant and refined 1953, cut from a very different cloth, but made in much the same way).

The evolution of more recent vintages of Léoville Barton is an interesting question. I’d suggest that the 1985 and 1988 (well worth looking for) are the best of the decade, followed by the 1989 and 1990 on pretty level pegging. The 1985 is the most attractive recent vintage that is drinking well.

Up until and including the (very strong) 1998, the wines seem to be very much in the school of the 1985. That made me wonder if the barrel programme had changed and is marking the wines more than it used to. I’d be curious to hear if anyone has anything to contribute on this subject, admittedly tangential to the thread.

posts.name: Brad B a l l i n g e r
posts.cooked: I drank from the same bottle as Craig. “Rustic” didn’t even come close to entering my mind. I believe this wine was also decanted and aerated a few hours before serving. It showed very well. And that was after some very good Burgundies.
posts.display_username: Brad B a l l i n g e r

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think we’re talking at crossed purposes. Rustic is a description of the style of the wine, not its tannins or evolution. In the same way that a Nuits St George Les Pruliers is more rustic than a Vosne Romanee Aux Reignots, or a Gruaud Larose is more rustic than a Pichon Lalande, or a 1982 Bordeaux is more rustic than a 1985, or Pegau is more rustic than Rayas, or whatever.
